data.title: Nvidia: Why The Permit Fears Don't Change Bullish Case
data.description:
Nvidia Remains A "Buy" After Its Q4 And Following The News On Requiring Permits

After my bullish upgrade on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) in mid-November, when the firm released its Q3 earnings, the stock price fell by over 7%, underperforming the broader market, which is a rarity for Nvidia since AI took over the headlines. Despite this lack of bullish momentum in the price action, the firm's financials remain solid - and it's not just about profitability. In fiscal Q4 2026, the company's total sales increased by 73% YoY to $68.13 billion, helping NVDA beat the consensus once again (by almost 3% or $1.90 billion, based on Seeking Alpha). On the bottom line, we've seen a YoY growth of over 82% (to $1.62), which was also above the consensus (by 5.32%).

The main market's concern, in my understanding, is about the longevity of hyperscaler CAPEX - we're now watching the widest "death jaw pattern," as Crescat Capital's analysts put it in their February note, when we match the mega-cap tech FCF flows with the S&P 500's (SPY, SP500) momentum:

In addition to that, the recent headlines regarding potential global U.S. export permits have put another layer of pressure on both NVDA and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) stocks.

Despite all the past and fresh fears around NVDA, I believe the stock is poised for continued strength as the management's plans play out and new offerings hit the market throughout the next few quarters.

There are a few reasons for staying positive on Nvidia's prospects.

First, the transition to the Blackwell architecture is progressing seamlessly, and it's already leading to strong financial results such as a profitability boost - in Q4, Nvidia's GAAP and adjusted GP margins expanded to 75.0% and 75.2%, respectively, and the ramp of the Blackwell architecture was cited as one of the main reasons for that margin expansion. The demand for Nvidia's chips remains so high that even older generations like Hopper and the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are still sold out in the cloud, per management's commentary.

With NVIDIA infrastructure in high demand, even Hopper and much of the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are sold out in the cloud. Nearly a year has passed since the release of our Grace Blackwell NVL72 systems. Today, nearly 9 gigawatts of infrastructure on Blackwell are deployed and consumed by the major cloud service providers, hyperscalers, AI model makers and enterprises.

Source: NVDA's Q4 earnings call.

From what I see, the firm's customers are absorbing its capacity as fast as Nvidia can produce it, and it can be seen from the Q4 data that the market decided to meet with skepticism.

Second, the risks that emerged following Bloomberg's news about export permits are likely phantom. In the article, it was revealed that the U.S. Commerce Department has drafted regulations that would restrict AI chip shipments globally without American approval. But thenm a spokesman came out with a post on X stating that the reports about them returning to the AI diffusion rule were not accurate.

The Commerce Department is committed to promoting secure exports of the American tech stack. We successfully advanced exports through our historic Middle East agreements, and there are ongoing internal government discussions about formalizing that approach. Today there was reporting that we were returning to the AI diffusion rule. We will not. It was burdensome, overreaching, and disastrous.

Source: U.S. Commerce Department's X post.

The Trump administration has repeatedly stated that it wants the world to use American AI, so it would be incorrect to assume that the new draft rules would function as an export ban on Nvidia. I agree that it's a geopolitical tool to ensure the U.S. wins in today's sovereign AI race, but that was a well-known fact before, so I think it was already priced into NVDA's stock long before Bloomberg's report came out. Also, it's important to understand that any draconian global bans on Nvidia's chips will only boost the attractiveness of foreign competitors like China's Huawei - that's not what the U.S. wants, obviously.

As long as Nvidia remains supply-constrained, not demand-constrained, any foreign entity's massive GPU order delays will simply be sold to a domestic hyperscaler or an enterprise customer, who are waiting in line.

While we expect tightness in the supply for our advanced architectures to persist, we remain confident in our ability to capitalize on the growth opportunity ahead with our scale, expansive supply chain and the long-standing partnerships continuing to serve us well.

Source: NVDA's CFO on the earnings call.

Third, Nvidia's lack of bullish reaction on what's already here - the agentic AI inflection point - doesn't make sense to me, as it should have repriced the stock to new heights on the Q4 call when the CEO introduced a new mantra: "Compute equals revenues":

The token generation rate is growing exponentially. And the same thing with, of course, OpenAI, their demand is incredible. And so the more compute that they can stand online, bring online, the faster their revenues will grow. And that goes back to the comment that I was saying that inference is revenues, that compute equals revenues now in this new world. And in a lot of ways, that's the reason why we say it's a new industrial revolution. There are new factories, new infrastructure being build and this new way of doing computing is not going to go back. And so, to the extent that we believe that producing tokens is going to be the future of computing, which I believe and I think largely the industry believes, then we're going to be building out this capacity from this point forward and continue to expand from here.

Source: NVDA's CEO on the Q4 call, emphasis added by Oakoff.

With agentic systems running continuously in the background, the demand for inference compute is skyrocketing, which is extremely bullish for cloud providers that can see the token activity translating into real dollars in real time, and it's indirectly bullish for Nvidia's racks and architectures as they're still the most power-efficient in the industry. I don't see NVDA losing this leading position any time soon. At CES 2026, they presented the Vera Rubin platform that should commence production shipments in fiscal 2H 2027 - this new architecture reduces inference token costs by up to 10x compared to Blackwell, according to management, and is likely to capture additional demand from enterprises and hyperscalers that are developing their AI agentic systems. So, Nvidia shouldn't become demand-constrained in the next few years, at least, in my understanding.

Fourth, following the negative news and after adjusting for the market's fears, NVDA has become relatively cheap when we match its valuation multiples with what's coming for its earnings/sales growth. According to Seeking Alpha's consensus data, NVDA's sales and EPS should grow at 5-year CAGRs of 11.6% and 11.2%, respectively (I took FY2027 as a base year for calculation). It might look modest compared to what is seen in the semis industry these days, but it's actually a lot for a $4.45 trillion market cap firm that a) has an undeniable moat in the hottest semis niche and b) trades at only ~17x forward earnings (fiscal FY2028):

In my November article, I used pretty bullish assumptions to figure out NVDA's fair value per share:

By the end of 2027, NVDA can keep its 40x earnings multiple in place, and if the trends in its sales and margins stay in place and NVDA keeps beating the consensus as it did before, we can assign a premium on its current EPS consensus, like I did in my previous update. With a 3-5% premium on their FY2027 EPS and with a 40x P/E ratio, my price target arrives at $277.89/share.

Today, I wouldn't go that bold. I'd take only 25-30x for FY2028 forward earnings because competition is on the rise from both semiconductor peers like AMD and in-house hyperscalers' solutions (Maia, Trainium, TPUs, etc.). Anyway, I'd take FY2028 consensus EPS with a slight premium of only 3-5% as I did last time to adjust for Nvidia's strong beating track record. All these updated inputs lead me to a 12-month target of $307.16/share:

$10.74 * 1.04 * 27.5x = $307.16/share (upside of +67%)

Source: Oakoff's calculations.

As you can see, despite the fact that I've taken a smaller exit P/E multiple for NVDA, the stock has become even more attractive in terms of its growth potential (last time I saw an undervaluation of ~46%).

With all the above bullish reasons in mind, I think Nvidia deserves to be rated a Buy after its prolonged consolidation.

Risks To Keep In Mind

As I noted last time, investors should understand that Nvidia is naturally at the epicenter of the AI boom, and it may be priced to perfection to some skeptics even if the stock trades at just 17x forward earnings. By buying NVDA stock right now, investors are effectively accepting a series of highly optimistic assumptions to justify Nvidia's $4.45 trillion valuation - if any of those assumptions fail to materialize, the forward 17x P/E can turn into 30-40x as the net income dips instead of rising higher. That would likely mean the ultimate break of the current bullish trend for NVDA.

Let's assume that NVDA's EPS grows by only 20% in FY2028 instead of 30.22% that the consensus currently prices in. In this scenario, the market is likely to assign a lower multiple, seeing a cyclical turn ahead - semis don't trade at extraordinary multiples historically because they're sensitive to cycles in general. So, if NVDA's FY2028 P/E drops to 15x, and the EPS itself comes in at ~$9.90, the resulting price target should be ~$148.5/share. It gives us a downside potential of about 19% to the current price.

Your Takeaway

The risks are high, and the narrative remains hammered by some events that I don't think can materially change my fundamentally bullish thesis on NVDA. I couldn't have thought we'd come to the point where buying NVDA is effectively a contrarian trade, but here we are.

In my opinion, the narrative should change when the market realizes that Nvidia's bullish cycle isn't over, and that the valuation has already become very tempting on a forward basis. It might be time to buy NVDA now as it's touching its 200-day moving average, and the consolidation seems to be taking too long.

In such cases, the stock should move up or down to interrupt the consolidation, and in my opinion, we have more reasons to expect a move up rather than a move down. I remain bullish on NVDA's prospects, keeping my Buy unchanged.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Playtika Is Trading At A Deep Discount To Its Cash Flow
data.description:
Playtika’s (PLTK) in a weird spot right now. The market just isn’t buying the story, even though Playtika’s cash flow is strong, margins are holding up and their push into direct-to-consumer and new games is actually working. Since I last said the stock looked like a buy, the shares have dropped another 19% to $2.92. That’s even though Playtika beat its adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2025 and continued to grow its direct-to-consumer and casual game businesses. The stock’s down almost 44% from the highs over the past year. People are still hung up on the old slot games and seem to doubt whether the company’s changes will stick, basically ignoring proof that profits and earnings are in better shape than the old numbers make it seem.

The Market Is Missing the Strength in Playtika’s Cash Flow

I’m still positive on the stock, mostly because the market’s pricing looks way off and the main things driving the business and cash are pretty clear. I’m pretty sure the new D2C push and new games will more than make up for old slot declines. The forward EV/EBITDA is about 3.6, price-to-cash-flow (TTM) around 1.8 and forward P/E at 3.7. All of those are really low, showing a lot of doubt. The implied cash flow yield is extremely high. With roughly $481.6 million in free cash flow against a market cap just over $1 billion, the stock trades at only about two times cash flow. That kind of valuation suggests the market is pricing in far more risk than the company’s recent results imply. The company expects 2026 revenue to be $2.7 to $2.8 billion, with adjusted EBITDA at $730 to $770 million. That’s a forward margin of 27 to 28%, which is right where it’s been historically. Even after adjusting for the non-cash SuperPlay earn-out accounting, GAAP net income remains depressed, but operating cash flow remained strong at $567.7 million in 2025, up from $490.1 million in 2024. That shows the core business is actually pretty stable.

The company isn’t just about slots anymore. Now, 74% of Q4 revenue came from casual games. The big thing now is direct-to-consumer: D2C made up 36.8% of revenue by the end of 2025 and it’s running at a $1 billion annual pace. Playtika still says it’ll get D2C to 40% in the next two years. This isn’t just a small shift - it’s a big change. Margins go up, and just as important, Playtika pays less to Apple (AAPL) and Google (GOOGL), which means they keep more of each dollar. Here’s how the company put it: “D2C keeps growing and adds more value for Playtika. In Q4, D2C was 36.8% of our revenues and we ended the year at about $1 billion in annualized D2C revenue” and “we are consistently optimizing those channels to improve unit economics and strengthen our business over time.”

What matters is this isn’t just a numbers trick. Actual players are moving over and sticking around longer. Management pointed out that player retention and the value of D2C users both went up and they drove home that “the advantage of having such a huge DTC platform is the connection, the right connection to the players... it will help with retention, it will help with long-time play game. So for us, this is one of the most important stuff.”

New Games Are Driving the Next Phase of Growth

The market isn’t really giving SuperPlay and Disney Solitaire enough credit in Playtika’s story. SuperPlay has quickly become a major growth driver for Playtika. Management said it’s “now our second largest game in the portfolio” and pointed out it’s getting a lot of traction in Japan, so there’s more room to grow internationally. Bingo Blitz and June’s Journey continue to generate meaningful revenue and remain two of the company’s most important franchises, helping offset the bigger drop in Slotomania. That loss was expected and is already being handled. The company has also taken steps to tighten its operations, including announcing a roughly 15% reduction in its workforce, as it shifts resources toward higher-growth games and direct-to-consumer channels.

Margins Are Holding Up Despite the Business Transition

Margins are holding up. Adjusted EBITDA for the year landed at $753.2 million, barely down (0.6%) even with all the changes going on. That’s a 27.3% adjusted EBITDA margin. Playtika has now hit or beaten its EBITDA guidance for three years running, which shows management is good at keeping costs in check and putting money where it counts. Capital spending is tight, with the company guiding for about $80 million in capital expenditures for 2026. They finished with $820.2 million in cash and equivalents. The net GAAP loss was $206.4 million. That number mostly comes from how they had to update the SuperPlay earn-out in their accounting. This made G&A expenses look a lot higher, but it’s really just a non-cash change in the numbers. It doesn't actually affect how the business works underneath.

Valuation is way out of sync with what’s actually happening in the business. Using consensus 2026 EPS of $0.57 and a pretty basic 10 to 12 times multiple, you get a fair value between $5.70 and $7.08. That’s about 95% to 140% higher than the current price. Even if you get super conservative and slap a low 7x multiple on that same EPS, assuming slots just keep fading, you still end up at $4, which is 37% above where it trades now. And that’s before even thinking about new game launches or more buybacks. The EV/EBITDA is around 4.3 and price-to-cash-flow is under 2, both way below what other similar companies get. The only way those numbers make sense is if this company just totally falls apart and keeps losing market share, but so far, nothing in the results points to that.

Investor Skepticism Remains High

People are still really down on the stock. The way the price has moved and how nobody seems to care about the actual numbers shows there’s a lot of skepticism baked in. And even when the company surprises to the upside, like with Disney or the D2C business, the market shrugs it off. Right now, most investors seem to think slot revenue is just going to keep dropping fast and nothing else will make up for it. The numbers don’t back that up. This gap between what’s being reported and what’s really going on financially is pretty normal when a company’s trying to turn things around. It sets up a shot at a big upside if/when the market finally notices the improvement in the business mix and steady EBITDA.

There are risks, but none of them are all-or-nothing. If the old slots business can’t stop shrinking, or if D2C growth stalls out, valuation multiples will probably get stuck on a shrinking cash flow base. If SuperPlay loses momentum, or if new games don’t launch well, both revenue and margins would take a hit. Changes in platform rules are less of a threat now because the company has built up its own D2C channels, but it’s always something to watch. The dividend is on hold, management wants to keep options open for deals and because of the SuperPlay earn-out, but buybacks are still on the table if cash flow holds up. Even if earnings drop 15-20% in a bad-case scenario, the stock still looks cheap on cash flow, though it probably wouldn’t get a higher multiple unless there’s real progress on stabilizing the business.

The Outlook Suggests Stability With Upside

The company’s outlook for 2026 is pretty concrete. Management is calling for $2.7-2.8 billion in revenue and $730-770 million in adjusted EBITDA and there’s a good amount of upside if D2C and SuperPlay/Disney Solitaire keep outperforming. On the downside, slots now make up less than a third of revenue, and the SuperPlay earn-out only adds some non-cash noise. To really prove the story, you’d want to see the D2C revenue mix hit or top 38-40% ahead of plan, Bingo Blitz and June’s Journey at least staying flat or growing, and Disney Solitaire holding or pushing past its current position as one of the company’s largest titles. On the flip side, if slot revenue keeps dropping and there’s nothing to offset it, if D2C migration slows, or if user acquisition costs jump a lot, the stock could get downgraded.

I’m sticking with a Buy. Playtika is still priced way below what its earnings power, margins and business mix shifts actually deserve. The numbers are right there. Unless we start seeing a string of failed new games, a cap on D2C, or negative free cash for a long stretch, the combination of steady cash flow, a more diverse portfolio and real scale in D2C and new titles makes this a Buy. The only way I’d change my mind is if free cash flow stays negative, margins dip below 25%, or D2C can’t move past 37% of revenue, none of which are happening. The market just isn’t seeing the staying power in D2C and new games yet and that’s where the opportunity sits.

data.title: Regencell Bioscience: Extended Lock-Up Creates Significant Upside Potential
data.description:
Regencell Bioscience Holdings Limited (RGC), a company focused on the development of Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) remedies for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) and Autism, is up a staggering 23,745% over the last year. The market capitalization now stands at $12.6b. This gives Chairman, CEO and Founder Yat-Gai Au a net worth of over $10b. Regencell has zero revenue and is unlikely to ever generate revenue.

All of the above is quite staggering and likely makes the title of this article seem all the more peculiar. Regencell is probably the most fundamentally overvalued business on the Nasdaq at this point in time, yet I believe the stock is likely heading a lot higher over the near term. I will discuss why in this article.

Stock is a vehicle for explosive moves

Regencell's upside potential comes down to float mechanics. 88.56% of the companies free float is owned by Yat-Gai Au or his wife. Year-after-year both of them committed to lock-up their shares, making them unable to sell any of their substantial holding. Therefore despite Yat's significant paper net worth, none of this is liquid, for now.

Regencell has grounds to become a meme stock and is already partially on its way there. The Company achieved a lot of attention across a range of social media platforms in June 2025 with large accounts like Gurgavin (458k followers) tweeting about the price action.

The Company was the biggest share gainer on the Nasdaq in 2025. However there is still grounds for increased attention in Wall Street Bets and across twitter. This would add fuel to the fire if Regencell starts to pick up price action momentum.

Reverse stock split

The stock briefly touched $80+ on 16 June, the day the company had executed a 35 for 1 stock split. The price became more accessible to a large number of investors which supported increased investor demand.

Both the Wall Street Journal and Bloomberg have now published news stories covering the run-up in stock price. The former even called the Company a "meme stock" in its' title.

What makes Regencell an outlier - maintaining its gains

Out of nearly every other meme stock runner or low-float stock, what makes Regencell a stark outlier is how the stock has held onto a large share of its gains. Whilst the huge spike in June was given back immediately, the company is still up 3,036% over the last year and has channeled between $10 and $60 for nine months.

Lets contrast this to AMTD Digital. In 2022, the French financial technology firm rallied more than 21,000% immediately following its listing. It then went and gave back all its over the following two months.

Regencell's differentiator is managements commitment to maintaining a low float and locking up shares which heightens volatility, creating asymmetric reward to the upside.

Extended lock-up:

What peaked my interest and led to my taking a (small) position in the stock was the under the radar SEC filing that was delivered on the 14 February 2026.

Regencell Bioscience Holdings Limited today announced that all employees and directors who were previously granted stock options have agreed to a further lock-up undertaking for an additional 12 months, until April 20, 2027.

This marks Regencell's fifth lock-up undertaking since Yat-Gai Au took the company public in July of 2021. The move reaffirms the commitment of management to maintaining a low float and supporting the elevated price, rather than attempting to sell off even a fraction of their holding at the former April 2026 lock-up expiry.

Significant risks:

All of Regencell's price action is based on market sentiment and mechanics. There are a number of significant risks which would impact a potential stock price run or lead to a substantial decline.

First, any future capital raise will dilute shareholders and undermine the low-float thesis. I would argue this is unlikely considering management's commitment to maintaining a low float. The business had $2.49m in cash on hand at the end of FY25. Based on last year's operating expenses of $3.1m, Regencell would run out of cash by 30 June 2026. However the business has short term investments of $2.48m. This should provide runway into 2027 - creating ample time for a low-float squeeze in the stock price.

The next update on the cash position will not be until June of this year, when Regencell files its interim report. The company operates under Cayman Islands law and qualifies as a Foreign Private Issuer (FPI). US listing rules require FPI's to file an interim report and annual report, the former of which is required within six months after the end of the half-year (Regencell's year end is the 31 December). Quarterly reporting is not required.

Regencell also disclosed a Department of Justice investigation in its FY25 financial results:

In addition, following recent volatility in the market for our Ordinary Shares, the Company received correspondence and a subpoena from the U.S. Department of Justice (“DOJ”), indicating that the DOJ is conducting an investigation into the trading in our Ordinary Shares.

Whilst this is only in the investigation phase, any future prosecution could lead to substantial shareholder losses or the suspension of shares.

The business also generates no revenue and is clearly significantly overvalued when performing any form of fundamental analysis. They have no approved, nor saleable products and have not applied for regulatory approval of any products. The company will generate losses for the foreseeable future.

This underscores the premise that this is a stock that is traded based on sentiment and near-term price action rather than any fundamental value. Hence a strong candidate for a meme stock.

Conclusion

Regencell Biosciences was the U.S. markets biggest "winner" in 2025, closing the year up a staggering 16,521%. Yet despite this the company has maintained its gains and is up 24% in the first two months of 2026.

The extremely low-float and managements commitment to maintaining this position, creates high volatility and provides room for significant upside (and downside) moves.

I have taken a small position following managements' extended lock-up, due to the stocks strong multi-bagger potential from current levels. The extension of the lock up period and short-term investments provides time for this to happen.

I am also comfortable and aware that I can lose all of my money through this investment, hence the small position sizing. I rate Regencell a short-term Buy.

data.title: Ooma: I'm Staying Bullish After Strong Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Thesis

I’ll be frank, when I decided to cover Ooma, Inc. (OOMA) in December, a small-cap cloud communications provider, I was skeptical. At first glance, the idea of its services, basically a company that helps people and businesses make phone calls using the internet instead of traditional phone lines, seemed a bit antiquated to me. Maybe too simple. Still, keeping an open mind, if the company’s service can hit both the residential and business markets, "simple" can be great. Even better: profitable.

Keeping an open mind, I ended up giving it a Buy rating and taking a small position (something I do when I publish a buy rating here, by the way), noting:

… I think OOMA’s discounted valuation and solid projected EPS growth make it look attractive, even after accounting for the familiar 'execution' risks tied to recent acquisitions and the intensifying competition in UCaaS.

And I’m glad I did. After percolating for a couple of months, shares finally came to life. Now, after going over the latest earnings, I'm maintaining a bullish stance as the company continues to show improving fundamentals and steady execution on its growth strategy.

My Take on Ooma, Inc’s Q4 FY2026 Results

  • Q4 FY2026 Revenue: $74.6M (+15% YoY)
  • Revenue from Acquisitions (Q4): $6.1M
  • FY2026 Total Revenue: $273.6M (+7% YoY)

Overall, I think the company performed well during the quarter. Revenue grew faster than before, driven mostly by recent acquisitions and particularly strong results from the AirDial segment. Management said the two smaller acquisitions, FluentStream and Phone.com, started generating meaningful revenue right away. These deals also shifted more of the company’s business toward higher-margin business subscription services, expanded its sales channels, and added another brand focused on small businesses. The company’s existing business (organic growth) also performed fairly well. However, most of the increase in total revenue came from the acquisitions rather than from the company’s existing operations.

  • Q4 FY2026 Adjusted EBITDA: $11.5M (15% margin)
  • FY2026 Adjusted EBITDA: $33.9M (12.4% margin)
  • FY2025 Adjusted EBITDA: $23.3M (9% margin)
  • Subscription Gross Margin: 72%
  • Product Gross Margin: −42%
  • Total Gross Margin: 63%

While acquisitions drove most of the revenue growth, they also had a noticeable impact on profitability. Profitability improved compared to the same quarter last year. The company’s adjusted EBITDA margin increased as the business benefited from operating leverage, mainly because it kept R&D and sales and marketing costs more tightly controlled. Meanwhile, gross margins for the subscription business stayed steady. This was despite a higher share of AirDial hardware installations, which still had negative margins. Even so, those margins improved compared to the previous quarter because older, more expensive inventory was used up and replaced.

  • TTM Operating Cash Flow: $27.7M
  • TTM Free Cash Flow: $22M
  • Stock Repurchases (last 4 quarters): $16.8M
  • Stock Repurchases (Q4): $4.6M
  • Term Loan for Acquisitions: $65M at 6.4% interest
  • Debt After Paydown: $58.5M

For the most part, OOMA focused on controlling costs and growing its higher-margin business revenue. This, in turn, helped it reach record levels of adjusted EBITDA and cash generation. The strong free cash flow allowed the company to pay down some of the debt (I always love to see this) taken on to finance the acquisitions and also carry out a small amount of share buybacks.

  • Product & Other Revenue (Q4): $5.9M (+30% YoY)
  • AirDial bookings growth (Q4): ~80% YoY
  • AirDial reseller partners: 41

Drilling down into the drivers of growth, as I mentioned earlier, AirDial delivered the strongest performance during the quarter. The number of new lines added reached a record level, and new customer bookings increased sharply. Management said this growth was mainly driven by rising prices and shutdown plans for POTS (Plain Old Telephone Service) lines. In particular, AT&T (T) has been increasing prices quickly and warning customers that more of these old phone lines will be retired in the future. Recent industry reports back that up. AT&T plans to shut down its copper phone network equipment in about 500 wire centers by the start of this summer, which represents a significant part of its network. The company also plans to continue phasing out copper lines more broadly, with the goal of retiring much of that infrastructure by 2029.

In a handful of markets, the monthly cost of POTS has climbed so high that it’s really no longer worth the trouble. In some cases, prices are now 200% to 400% higher than they used to be. Naturally, because of these increases, many businesses can no longer afford to keep using these older phone lines and are starting to switch to newer alternatives.

This positive trend is expected to continue through the current year, especially since Verizon (VZ) and other companies have not yet increased similar efforts, which, when you connect the dots, definitely leaves a lot of room for growth. Keep in mind, Ooma’s partner network has grown to 41 resellers, and some of these partners have moved over from competing companies. That suggests to me that Ooma's holding its own in the market.

Before we get too excited, hang tight. From management's mouth, the AirDial opportunity is still somewhat "lumpy", since large customer deployments can heavily influence when installations actually happen.

CEO Eric Stang explained:

AirDial grows in a couple of ways. There is a steady stream of business we know or can reasonably forecast we're going to drive every quarter… But there's also big deals out there, larger size deals. And they're lumpy and you don't know when a customer is going to pull the trigger....But because it's lumpy and because 1 customer can be 5,000 or 10,000 lines ultimately, if it's a very large customer, you just don't know when you're going to win those and who's going to win those. So we're a little more conservative on how we forecast AirDial today.

  • ARPU: $15.99 (+5% YoY)
  • Office Pro / Pro Plus adoption (new users): 57%
  • Total Office users on Pro / Pro Plus: 39%

Outside of AirDial, in the main Ooma Office business, more customers are choosing the higher-priced Pro and Pro Plus plans. This shift helped increase ARPU (Average Revenue Per User). And, no surprise here, upcoming AI features, including call transcription, conversation summaries, integrated insights, AI answering, and full receptionist capabilities, are expected to encourage customers to upgrade to these higher-tier plans even more. Of course, the success of these additions will depend if customers will take the bait, so to speak. In other words, it all depends if customers feel that the features are valuable enough to justify paying additional fees or upgrading to higher-priced plans.

  • Residential subscription revenue change (Q4): −1% YoY

In the residential part of the business, it sustained its competitiveness. Telo units brought in new users even though there were the usual seasonal slowdowns. Management said the home phone market is changing for several reasons. Aside from the POTS phase out, there's the 5G home internet which is making it easier for people to separate services instead of buying them together in bundles. At the same time, many parents are looking for screen-free phones for younger children.

Personally, I love the sound of this trend, considering I grew up during a time where I had to hunt down a pay phone if I needed my mom to pick me up after tearing up the roller skating rink.

I'll let CEO Stang explain:

There's a movement happening among parents to wait until eighth grade before letting a child receive a smartphone. Ooma's Family Bundle, consisting of the Ooma Telo and a family-friendly phone, is one way families use our solutions. In fiscal 2027, we intend to launch a new product called My Phone, which we hope parents will find particularly attractive for use by younger people in the home. We'll have more to say on this as our strategy unfolds.

Taking all of these segments together, cash flow stayed strong and was used to pay down debt and fund share buybacks without putting pressure on the company’s liquidity. Management also said it is open to doing more small tuck-in acquisitions in the fragmented UCaaS (Unified Communications as a Service) market, where smaller companies can fit well with Ooma’s size and operations.

OOMA’s Valuation Assessment

OOMA appears to trade well below its estimated fair value. At the current price, it's below the ~$21 fair value estimate, which is based on normalized P/E multiples.

The forward non-GAAP P/E ratio is about 10x, which looks attractive considering the company's expected to grow EPS by about 29%.

Other valuation measures also tell me the stock's inexpensive, with EV to forward sales around 1.1x and EV to EBITDA at about 8x compared to peers.

Be aware that OOMA's debt is equal to about 39% of its capital.

Not bad as long as they maintain a good handle on that leverage. Even so, the current free cash flow's strong enough to support those share buybacks and the outlook is still appealing because EBITDA growth and strong performance through sales channels suggest the business could keep improving.

Final Verdict

I'm keeping my Buy rating on OOMA as the overall risk-reward appears to favor the upside. The shares trade at about 10x forward earnings, while EPS is expected to grow nearly 30%. The company also benefits from long-term demand for AirDial as POTS lines are shut down.

If you're looking to time an entry, we could see some near-term selling considering how spooked the market is with this new "war." Technically, in terms of resistance levels, getting through the $16 level could be a challenge (the RSI is also in that overbought area now), but if/once that breaks, expect that to become the new support like we saw in 2021-22.

data.title: 3M: Stabilizing Fundamentals Support A Hold Upgrade
data.description:
I had issued a Sell rating on 3M (MMM) in May last year on litigation, tariff and growth worries. The litigation and growth worries are still applicable, but have not worsened as I had feared in May last year. Importantly, the tariff implications have played out better than anticipated. The operational execution and earnings trajectory looks more investible today.

3M is now ~6% up in terms of total returns since the time of my Sell call and has not seen any material downside, potentially supported by a broader bull market phase we saw since then too. 3M's relative drag is visible compared with broader market returns - the S&P 500 has been up ~16% since May last year. At the time of the Sell call, I did not see enough proof of stability to bet on 3M as a growth investment, neither income. I see enough stability now to upgrade the thesis to a Hold. The income potential is still not lucrative enough to compensate for the growth risks, but the stock has become a little more predictable based on several fundamental improvements (or lack of worsening).

The Tariff Headwinds

Tariffs were one of the central talking points in May 2025. Management had estimated a very large tariff impact of up to ~$850m annualized, and I was worried about the uncertainties around whether that too was conservative in an evolving regime. The EPS impact before mitigation was pegged at ~$0.6, with around $0.2-0.4 estimated mitigation - material impact and an unknown net effect at that point.

In Q4, 3M has estimated the drag to be ~$140m (mostly the first half of the year) and there could be another $30-40m in tariffs later from new Europe tariffs. The sum total still looks less than a third of the worst case scenarios we were looking at in May last year. Importantly, we now know the real impacts and have hard numbers to be able to model impacts more confidently ahead - the degree of uncertainty is lower. Supply chain adjustments have been implemented and targeted price actions are also implemented. The tariff regime is also potentially in a thawing cycle now. This is the single most important factor that changes the more harsh Sell call issued earlier.

Earnings and Growth Exceeds Expectations

The EPS guidance at the time of the Sell call was ~$7.75 at the midpoint for 2025. The actual number we have for the year now is ~$8.06 - this is higher than the upper end of the guidance and represents a double digit EPS growth YoY.

Another important and positive change is that 3M is now consistently outperforming the industrial macro environment. According to 3M, while the macro is pegged at ~1.5% growth in 2025, 3M's organic growth has been ~60 bps higher. Management has attributed this to commercial execution improvements including pricing discipline, collaborative planning with customers and increased cross-selling. The 2026 outlook is even stronger with 3M likely to see ~3% organic growth (130 bps above macro). Overall revenues were at a local trough in Q1 2025 last year and has not worsened since due to the organic growth holding up - in fact barring Q4, it did show steady improvements. Q4 was dragged by a consumer segment decline, but the industrial business remained solid. And a stronger organic growth outlook for 2026 means we are not looking at any material revenue decline scenarios, like we were in May 2025.

I believe the growth outperformance relative to macro growth is being supported by innovation. The number of new products reported in 2025 was 284 - double the levels seen in 2023 and 68% more than the new product launches we saw in 2024. Sales from products launched in the past five years rose 23% in 2025. This is expected to remain supportive in 2026 too, with around 360 launches expected.

Margins were previously a concern given tariff exposure and litigation costs. Instead, operating leverage has improved. Full year adjusted operating margins in 2025 were up by 200 bps YoY at 23.5%. This can be attributed to structural factors like supply chain and productivity improvements, as well as administrative cost efficiencies. For 2026, management expects an additional 70-80 bps margin expansion.

Not a Buy

The thesis may look like a bullish call on 3M. It is not. The improvements highlighted only support an upgrade to a Hold (not a Buy) because a lot of factors are still the same. Of course, "still the same" is decent for a Hold because I was expecting worsening in growth and margin - so even moderate stable positives suffice to move to a Hold. However, a Buy requires a more asymmetrical setup - which we don't have yet. While margins are still reasonably strong, the growth indications for 2026 still indicate a phase of slow compounding at best.

In this slow compounding scenario, what tilts a thesis like 3M's toward a Buy (even if for income) is a valuation comfort that indicates a supportive floor. The forward EV to EBITDA ratio today is ~12.6x - around the sector median and still above its 5-year average. It is climbed down from ~13.25x levels seen in May last year despite better than expected earnings, but the multiple compression is still moderate. We do not have enough to chase 3M on growth grounds and we do not have enough comfort from the valuations to be able to chase a ~2% yield.

A key reason I do not see enough merit to bet on growth is not only a tempered (though positive) guidance for 2026, but end-market weakness. A slowdown in housing and renovation activity continues to weigh on demand. Although new product launches will likely keep 3M ahead of this weak spending environment, a cyclical push will be required before 3M can become a Buy.

Another key risk that weighed on 3M's thesis earlier was litigation risk. That has not changed - it still remains a structural risk for the company. Litigation costs are more predictable now and no material new risk in that regard has been added though. 3M has indicated that litigation costs will remain around the current annual levels of ~$500m. I still feel the litigation risks warrant a valuation discount that is not sufficiently reflected in today's valuation .

Overall, the upgrade from Sell to Hold is based on operational strength (or rather lack of any further weakness from the uncertainties of May last year) and no new risk added. However, I would still wait for a fresh catalyst for a Buy - the nearest material catalyst will need to come from external factors like the macro turning favorable and boosting growth. That is not evident today, and will need to be watched.

data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper: Despite The Business Transformation Risks, I Am A Buyer
data.description:
Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) is an international manufacturer and distributor of beverages. The firm sells its products under brand names like Dr. Pepper, Canada Dry, or A&W, just to mention a few.

While one may consider KDP a defensive name and therefore a suitable investment during times of low consumer confidence, the firm's share price actually performed quite poorly over the past 12 months. KDP lost 15% of its market value over the period, underperforming both the consumer staples sector (XLP) and the broader market (SPY). KDP's poor performance in the past months was largely driven by the negative sentiment around the JDE PEET's deal.

The aim of my article today is to look at KDP's latest earnings report, as well as its valuation, to assess whether this underperformance is likely to persist or not.

Earnings

In the most recent quarter, KDP beat analyst estimates on the top and bottom lines. Revenue came in at $4.45B - $100 million above expectations, representing a constant currency net sales growth of 9.9%. EPS came in at $0.6, $0.01 above expectations and 1.7% higher than in the same period in the previous year.

If we break down the results by reported segments, we can see that each of them contributed to the revenue growth. In general, I like to see such broad-based growth, as it allows the firm to have a more diversified revenue stream rather than relying on a single product or product category.

In the U.S. Refreshment beverages segment, both volume, mix, and pricing contributed to the growth. Volume grew by as much as 7%, while net price realization contributed 4.5%. The increase in volume can be traced back to market share gains, as well as to the acquisition of GHOST, which contributed 6.2% to the volume growth figure. While adjusted operating income grew, the profitability was hit by increased SG&A expenses and inflationary pressures.

Inflationary pressures, especially the price of raw materials, can keep having significant downward pressure on the margins in the coming quarters. The following chart shows the aluminum prices over the past 12 months. Commodity prices, even if partially hedged, can create downward pressure on the profitability. In my view, they remain one of the risk factors that investors need to keep an eye on.

The U.S. Coffee segment also posted positive sales growth, mainly driven by the 8% price realization, partially offset by the declining volume - 4.1%. K-Cup pod sales impacted the quarterly revenue growth positively, but they were offset partially offset by the decline in brewer sales. Due to inflationary pressures and volume/mix, the segment's adjusted operating income declined by as much as 8.8%, while the margin compressed by 4.3 bps.

I believe that the margin pressures may ease in the coming quarters. Coffee prices have normalized over the past few months and are much lower now than where they were in Q4.

The international segment - the firm's smallest segment - performed the best in the past quarter. Revenue grew by as much as 16%, adjusted operating income grew by 20%, and the adjusted operating margin expanded by 0.8 points. Volume/mix contributed 6.8% to the revenue growth, while net price realization added 9.2%. The operating income growth was primarily driven by the topline growth, complemented with increased productivity.

All in all, I believe that the firm's strong quarterly results, demonstrating strong demand for the products, make the firm attractive from a fundamental point of view. Risks related to commodity prices need to be kept in mind, however. And before continuing to the

Looking forward, KDP expects sales growth to continue in the mid-single-digit region, while adjusted EPS is in the low-double-digit region. Keep in mind that the acquisition of JDE Peet's is a significant contributor to the growth. In terms of net sales, $8.5 to $8.7 billion is expected to be generated by JDE Peet's in 2026, while it is anticipated to contribute 6-7 points to the EPS growth.

There is one significant risk that we need to take into account going forward. And this is the acquisition of JDE Peet's. The deal is planned to be finalized by April 2026, and it has quite a few consequences. First, the long-term debt is going to significantly increase, leading to potentially higher interest expenses, which could lead to a margin contraction. While management promises rapid deleveraging, I would like to see it being actually executed, not just promised. Second, the company is foreseen to be split into two: Beverage Co. and Global Coffee Co. Such a restructuring also comes at a cost, which is likely going to bite into the profitability metrics. At the same time, $400 million in cost synergies is expected.

Valuation

KDP's business appears attractive fundamentally, despite the risks related to the business transformation ahead. But we also have to ask ourselves, is the current share price the right price to pay? The following table summarizes a set of traditional price multiples and compares KDP's figures with those of the consumer staples sector median, as well as with its own historic averages. When we look at the comparison of current vs. historic metrics, we can see that KDP is selling at a significant discount.

In my view, this discount is not justified. I do understand that the firm's profitability somewhat deteriorated compared to the past year due to inflationary pressures and higher SG&A costs, but the firm's growth remains strong both top- and bottom-line.

Further, if we narrow down the peer group to key players in the industry, we can see that according to most valuation metrics, KDP is one of the cheapest companies.

I believe that at the current price, KDP is an attractive opportunity for both value and growth investors.

Before concluding this section, I would also like to highlight the company's commitment to returning value to its shareholders. Over the past years, KDP remained a reliable dividend payer. The quarterly dividend is currently $0.23 per share, equivalent to an annual yield of 3.2%. These payments are well-covered by the cash flow from operations. Further, KDP also bought back some shares over the past years. While the share buybacks were not very consistent, they still created some value for the shareholders.

Last, but not least, I also need to note that the last bigger insider selling was back in August 2025, when the share price was roughly $33, compared to the current $28.5.

Conclusions

KDP delivered strong results in the most recent quarter, beating analyst estimates top- and bottom-line and posting a year-over-year revenue growth of 9.9% in constant currency and an EPS growth of 1.7%.

The demand for KDP's products remained strong across all reported segments, with each category growing. Margin pressures were significant in the U.S. coffee segment.

The business transformation remains a key risk going forward.

KDP appears attractive from a valuation point of view. The valuation multiples appear attractive compared to historic averages as well as to industry peers. The lack of significant insider selling, as well as the attractive dividends, makes the stock even more attractive from a valuation perspective.

The key risks related to KDP are input costs and consumer preferences.

All in all, I believe KDP is an attractive investment opportunity at the current price.

data.title: Oil Prices Will Dictate When This War Ends
data.description:
There was nowhere to hide yesterday, as stocks, bonds, precious metals, and cryptocurrencies all fell with oil prices continuing their meteoric rise. The major market indexes staged a rally in the final hour of the day, as WTI crude oil backed off its intraday high of more than $82 to approximately $79.50. Prior geopolitical conflicts like this one resulted in stock market bottoms coinciding with peaks in oil prices, and I think this one will be no different. The question is where will the peak be this time. This conflict is in its seventh day.

Weekly unemployment claims edged lower to 213,000 last week, indicating that we remain in a no hire, no fire labor market in advance of this morning’s jobs numbers for February. A better-than-expected ADP payroll report in combination with the strongest ISM Services PMI since 2022 suggests that job growth exceeded the 50,000 expected, but this would be a small consolation for a market that is myopically focused on the ramifications of the attack on Iran. Investors are concerned that a sustained rise in oil prices will reignite inflation and prevent the Fed from lowering interest rates later this year. I share that sentiment, which is why I think oil will dictate when this conflict ends.

The S&P 500 has a nearly perfect inverse correlation with oil prices right now, as the index rallied yesterday at the precise time that crude oil fell abruptly from more than $82 to under $80. This suggests that so long as the price of oil keeps rising the S&P 500 will continue sliding. As of this morning. WTI futures are trading above $86, and the S&P 500 is flirting with yesterday’s lows. I don’t think this is lost on the Trump administration even though no one will admit it.

It also has to be concerning to the President that gasoline prices are rising sharply with the national average surging to $3.25/gallon yesterday. The “affordability” issue is top of mind for consumers, and the majority in every poll available oppose the attack on Iran. This builds tremendous pressure on members of Congress who are running for reelection this fall, and the President who wants his party to retain control.

Then there is the headwind from rising interest rates, as investors sell Treasury bonds over fears that inflation will reignite and prevent the Fed from lowering short-term interest rates. This also runs counter to the efforts to address affordability, as rising 10-year Treasury yields drive up mortgage rates. After falling below 4%, the 10-year yield has risen every day this week to 4.14%.

I think this has the Trump administration looking for the exit ramp from this conflict in the coming days. I focus less on what administration officials say, which is often posturing, and far more on what they are likely to do. The question is how high do oil prices and interest rates have to go to force an about face. Considering how quickly the administration reacted to the tariff tantrum last April, I think it is sooner than later, which means sometime next week. Despite assurances of safe passage, the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed with traffic collapsing by more than 95% since the attack began. With limited options for some 15 million barrels per day that pass through this narrow channel, this is the choking point for the global economy. It is also cutting off the supply and driving up the cost of other input materials from fertilizers to the raw materials needed to manufacture semiconductor chips. The current situation is simply untenable.

Any indication that the bombing has concluded or negotiations are planned should lead to a plunge in oil prices and a strong rally in risk assets. At that point, it is time to assess whether the damage is short term or has longer term implications for our economy and markets.

data.title: Altimmune Reports FY25 Financials: Why Is Big Pharma Staying Away
data.description:
Thesis

Altimmune, Inc. (ALT) has just reported a 4Q25 GAAP EPS of -$0.27, a figure which missed analyst expectations by about $0.03. Now, even with the earnings miss, the company did still manage to post some revenue, about of $0.03 million, which actually exceeded the consensus by about the same margin. It was also a bit of an improvement over the $5,000 in revenue we saw last year. The company also managed to set up a pretty decent cash position at $274 million going into 2026. We also got an update on some significant operational progress on the call, especially surrounding pemvidutide. As a reminder, the candidate is a dual glucagon/GLP-1 receptor agonist being developed for some pretty serious liver diseases, such as MASH.

Management is expecting 2026 to be a pivotal year for the program. The goal here would be to build on the encouraging Phase 2b IMPACT trial data we saw back in December last year. If you remember, it demonstrated statistically significant improvements in non-invasive markers of liver fibrosis/inflammation. Now, Altimmune has plans to initiate a global Phase 3 registrational trial this year. Importantly, however, we just saw pemvidutide get Breakthrough Therapy Designation from the FDA, so the regulators’ view here is that the drug may offer meaningful clinical improvements over existing treatments for MASH. We can also expect the Phase 3 study to evaluate multiple doses over a 52-week treatment period via biopsy-based endpoints.

Financials

Coming now to the financials, even though the stock slid about %17 on the call, my view was that the update was fairly solid. Financially, Altimmune strengthened its balance sheet a lot during 2025 and even early 2026 to help out with the upcoming late-stage clinical development. The company managed to end FY25 with about $274 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. This sum is more than double the $132 million they reported at the end of 2024. However, as of February 28th of this year, that total cash sum has risen to roughly $340 million thanks to that $75 million registered direct offering and some additional ATM equity sales.

As for spending, as you would expect, operating expenses were, for the most part, driven by research and development, in particular costs tied to pemvidutide’s programs. For 4Q25, R&D hit $18.4 million, actually slightly lower than last year’s period because of the timing of clinical research organisation costs. Elsewhere, general and administrative expenses rose slightly to $10.5 million due to some higher professional fees and compensation related to the leadership transitions. Overall, Altimmune reported a net loss of about $27.4 million for 4Q25, slightly higher than the $23.2 million we saw back in 4Q24. For FY25, they posted a net loss of $88.1 million, which was an improvement from the $95.1 million loss back in 2024. Importantly, though we saw liquidity increase, and if we take their FY25 operation spend as a proxy for cash burn, it gives them a decent runway size of about 2.5 years, taking them well into 2028. We are certainly seeing the company set up capital resources to fund that costly Phase 3 MASH program and some other clinical milestones expected in 2026.

Pemvidutide progress

So Altimmune gave a large portion of the earnings call for updates on pemvidutide. As you know, they have a planned Phase 3 program in MASH. Management said we can expect the study to begin this year and should enrol about 1,800 patients globally. The participants will, of course, have biopsy-confirmed MASH and liver fibrosis. We should keep in mind that this trial is being designed specifically to meet FDA registrational endpoints, which we typically see needing to demonstrate either resolution of MASH without worsening of fibrosis or somekind of improvement in fibrosis without worsening of MASH. This will be after about 52 weeks of treatment.

Just to touch briefly on the mechanism here, pemvidutide is essentially designed as a dual agonist of the GLP-1 and glucagon receptors. So activation of the GLP-1 pathway helps reduce appetite, as you know, with all the semaglutid drugs now on the market, but it also improves glucose control, as well as driving weight loss. On the other hand, the glucagon receptor is being activated, which should increase energy expenditure and enhance lipid metabolism in the liver. So it’s a dual activity intended to both reduce liver fat accumulation and improve systemic metabolic health. So in theory, this could address quite a few aspects of MASH simultaneously. The drug is tipped to be administered as a once-weekly subcutaneous injection, similar to other incretin-based metabolic drugs on the market. So it makes it practical for chronic treatment if approved, but it also means we need a differentiating factor here, as I'll explain.

The goal of the Phase 3 trial here is to confirm that pemvidutide can simultaneously address both the underlying liver disease and the key metabolic drivers. This is also the key differentiator when it comes to pemvidutide that investors are hoping to see. And because Phase 3 MASH trials require histological endpoints measured by liver biopsies, meaningful data can take a while to generate. So based on the expected 2026 start and the duration of treatment and follow-up, top-line results would likely arrive sometime around 2028 or later, in line with their cash runway. Even though it’s a long way out, we would hope to see statistically significant improvements in fibrosis and disease resolution, as well as strong weight-loss effects. Of course, clean safety data is also a wanted outcome since tolerability has been a limiting factor for some metabolic therapies in the past.

Partnership concerns still remain

So we know that the Phase 3 trial results are still very far away, and the big concern for investors right now is that despite the hefty amount of capital flowing into metabolic disease assets over the past few years, Altimmune isn’t able to secure a meaningful partnership to help out with pemvidutide development. What’s made this worse is that the company has been talking about it for a while, and yet nothing concrete seems to come. Also, over the last 18 to 24 months, we’ve been seeing some very large pharmaceutical companies pay anywhere from $3 billion to $10 billion for early-stage obesity and MASH programs. And in a good few of those cases, big pharma paid up for assets that were only in Phase 1 or early Phase 2. Altimmune, on the other hand, has pemvidutide, which now has an FDA Fast Track designation and Breakthrough Therapy designation, along with some strong 24-week and 48-week clinical data. But no strategic partner or even interest yet. It’s a bit puzzling here, especially since management always describes pemvidutide as being capable of addressing multiple high-value indications. So these include MASH, Obesity, Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease, and even Alcohol Use Disorder. We even been told that some broader cardiometabolic complications could be possible.

So investors, as well as I, are obviously thinking that if the opportunity were truly as compelling as management describes, there would be at least some level of participation from big pharma. This doesn’t actually need to be an acquisition it could just be an equity investment, a co-development deal, or even a licensing agreement. Instead, we saw Altimmune have to raised $75 million from a private institutional investor. Now, in doing that, it doesn't help with the concerns. Why would strategic buyers, who collectively have hundreds of billions in deployable capital for investments such as these, have not yet stepped in? Especially since were seeing somewhat of a wave of acquisitions/partnerships in the obesity and liver disease space. Some pharma companies have actually been aggressively trying to establish any kind of foothold in this space.

Now, at the same time, the whole competitive landscape is already shifting and starting to settle around a few early leaders, which makes Altimmune's position look increasingly left behind. For example, Madrigal Pharmaceuticals just successfully brought Rezdiffra to market as the first FDA-approved treatment for MASH with fibrosis. And now, large pharmaceutical companies have already started aligning themselves with established or late-stage programs. So the longer Altimmune goes here without a strategic partner, the more difficult I think it could get for them, with the market becoming so crowded. Which is why I stressed on them needing some kind of solid differentiator in the space.

Now, the big concern is the Phase 3 trial timeline. By the time we see it through, I expect a number of other metabolic therapies, particularly those with decent next-generation GLP-1 combinations, to may already be targeting the same patient populations that Altimmune is looking at. So if you’re an investor here looking at this setup, I don't blame you for seeing too much risk here in the sense that pemvidutide could arrive late to a very rapidly evolving market. Especially since the larger players naturally have the advantage in terms of commercial infrastructure and regulatory momentum. The big argument from management is that pemvidutide has that differentiating potential, in that it can act as a dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor mechanism, so offer unique metabolic/liver benefits that competitors can't. But if that were the case, and big pharma, who have people far more educated in the space than us havent though to take a closer look. So it’s contradictory, management claims differentiation and yet no look-in from big pharma still raises our concerns that either the data are not as differentiated as we would like or that management’s track record on negotiating could be limiting deal opportunities

Will heaper GLP-1’s end up dominating

Another issue I see here is the rapid commoditization risk of the GLP-1 class itself. We know that the key intellectual property around the early GLP-1 drugs is going to expire soon, and thus, a lot of cheaper alternatives should start making an impact on the market. For example, the big case is the patents around semaglutide; the patent is expected to roll off later in the decade, depending on jurisdiction/formulation protections. So one viewpoint here is that once the true generics or biosimilar versions begin to emerge, the bigger economics of the obesity/metabolic drug market are going to shift dramatically. So, for those thinking about holding Altimmune stock through until then, you have to consider that those large pharmaceutical companies want to enter the space as cheaply as possible. So they’re going to try to acquire or license the simpler GLP-1 assets or even potential generic formulations rather than paying multi-billion-dollar premiums for an experimental dual- or even the triple-agonist molecules that still require years of very expensive Phase 3 development that they’d have to help out with. So in that context, the big pharma mindset is going to change over the next couple of years, in that instead of chasing every new next-gen incretin formulation, they may just prioritise speed to market and cost advantages. This is particularly true when you consider they will have to manufacture at scale and have to deal with payers and healthcare systems that are already starting to struggle with the hefty price tags of the branded obesity drugs.

There is also a broader clinical reality concern here, in that Altimmune's potential differentiator may not be as valuable as they think. The reality is that most GLP-1–based therapies ultimately have some kind of side effect profile once you reach the doses needed for meaningful effect. Whether the drug is just a single GLP-1 agonist or a more complex dual/triple receptor agonist, patients will inevitably experience some kind of gastrointestinal side effects. It could be nausea, vomiting, or diarrhoea. We even saw that newer drugs like tirzepatide from Eli Lilly are still reporting meaningful rates of GI intolerance in clinical trials, but the focus from the market is mainly on the impressive weight-loss efficacy.

So the real-world differentiation here going forward may change, and actually be far smaller than clinical trial presentations would suggest. Especially once we see them get used across broader patient populations outside the tightly controlled studies. So the way I would phrase the question here is, does the industry actually need dozens of slightly different GLP-1 combinations, or would a widely available low-cost GLP-1 backbone therapy just end up dominating the market?

Valuation

If you look at the valuation profile, things seem quite expensive. Altimmune really trades more like a clinical-stage biotech here rather than a commercial pharma firm, which makes the traditional valuation metrics difficult to apply, especially with the tiny revenue figure. The company currently has a market cap of about $439 million and an enterprise value of about $200 million. They have that sizable cash position relative to their size to help out here. And with virtually no commercial revenue, we're seeing ratios appearing extremely elevated. Just for some context on their cash flow, the spending here is mostly reflected in cash used in operating activities, and in that sense, the big spend is R&D and the upcoming clinical trial costs for pemvidutide. Capital expenditures are only minimal since the company doesn't really operate a large manufacturing infrastructure. It means that liquidity runway is mostly determined by operating cash outflows.

More relevant would be the price-to-book ratio of about 1.73x, which is just slightly below the healthcare sector median of about 2.8x. So it would suggest that the stock is trading at only a modest premium to its underlying equity base. Ultimately, I feel the valuation for Altimmune is less about current financial metrics and way more dependent on the future clinical/regulatory upside. We have to realistically price in the contribution of pemvidutide we could potentially see.

Looking ahead

So moving forward, I think the bigger picture here is pretty clear. ALtimmune are moving into Phase 3 and is pretty well funded. The financials on earnigs was that bad at all, even though we saw a 17% slide. It could well be the case that investors were hoping for management to announce some outside interest on the call. So take in the face that big pharma deals occur after the end of phase 3, but usually but before Phase 3 initiation, it does seem Altimmune is getting somewhat left behind. For example, Pfizer’s acquisition of Metsera was just this, paying up billions for a late‑stage obesity/GLP‑1 asset at the Phase 3 cusp. Either way, the upcoming Phase 3 start for pemvidutide is the key near-term catalyst, whether we see outside interest or not.

data.title: Costco Thrives And The Cash Pile Builds (Earnings Review)
data.description:
Costco Is Roaring Back

Costco (COST) keeps surprising investors. It was only a few years ago that we saw Warren Buffett selling his whole position after two decades of ownership because of valuation concerns. People now calculate that he left over $1.5 billion on the table, and he himself admitted that selling Costco was probably a mistake.

We have discussed many times how to behave with this stock, which is held by a cohort of shareholders who are very reluctant to let go of their shares. Even when the stock dips a bit, it still trades at high multiples, and this makes many investors pass on the opportunity to initiate a position. We saw the stock trade more or less sideways for almost two years. Yet, YTD, Costco is back to its ATHs above $1,000. In a turbulent world, where investors haven't fully made up their minds on whether AI is an opportunity or a threat and where conflict outbreaks increase fear and concern, Costco immediately stands out as the go-to, reliable, and safe pick.

There are several reasons why Costco's business model is so strong and appreciated by the market. We can summarize it by saying that Costco is not really a retailer that sells goods, but a buyer on behalf of its members, who can sum up their individual bargaining power and, through Costco, obtain the best possible deals on a selected number of goods. Costco's economics, therefore, rely on top-line expansion (as most retailers do), but this is a proxy for its expansion plan and its members' commitment and engagement. This is why its true hidden gem is the membership program. When we understand that its profits come, for the most part, from its membership fees, we understand that we can truly assess the company through one metric: gross membership income, as I explained some time ago in this article. It shows that Costco is actually more similar to a subscription business under the disguise of a retailer.

Costco is renowned for its ability to constantly identify good deals for its members. Remember when Costco began selling gold bars back in 2024, when prices were around $2,000/oz? Well, that was a real deal that Costco struck for its members, and those who bought those bars have seen them go up 150% in value since their purchase. Now, Costco didn't sell gold bars to make more profits, but by selling gold bars at a competitive price, it was able to attract more customers to its warehouses, increasing its membership subscriptions. The same is true for the $1.50 hot dog. Costco loses money on it (hot dogs nowadays cost at least $4.50), but it attracts and retains members thanks to this offer.

Thanks to its growing membership income, Costco generates billions in annual FCF while also receiving upfront hoards of cash at no interest. This enables Costco to run without relying on debt and expand its business thanks to easy access to liquidity. To Costco, debt is an optionality, not a need. Moreover, the company only reports $5.67 billion in LT debt, while it holds over $16.2 billion in cash. Since Costco invests around $5.7 billion in CapEx, the company could easily pay off all its debt and still have enough cash to fund its CapEx while also retaining over $5B at hand. This is why Costco often pays a special dividend, and many expect a new one to be announced within the year.

With this in mind, let's now look at Costco's Q2 FY26 earnings, which the company just released, where investors were not waiting only for the financials, but also for an update on the tariff refund process.

Costco's Q2 Earnings Review

Costco just reported its Q2 earnings, and we see, once again, that its sales grew by 9.1% YoY to $68.24 billion (Q2 2025: $62.53 billion). This growth was driven by a 3.1% YoY growth in traffic and a 4.2% increase in tickets. This result is accretive to the 1H print, which reports revenues at $134.22 billion, up 8.7% YoY. The U.S. grew 6.1% ex gas and FX, while Canada is up 8.3% in the first half, and international is up 7%. What can we say? Sometimes, Costco appears so predictable that it could be perceived as boring. But this is precisely Costco's value: its steady compounding strength has been able to push the stock to where it is now.

Costco's e-commerce sales are also picking up speed, reporting 21.7% YoY growth.

The quarterly net income was $2.04 billion, which is equal to $4.58 per share, a 13.6% YoY increase (Q2 2025: $4.03). This means that Costco reported both a top- and a bottom-line beat.

Membership fee revenue was $1.36 billion for the quarter, while the 24-week result is $2.68 billion.

It is noteworthy to say that Costco's cash balance is now $17.4 billion. Considering that the company will deploy around $5.6-$5.8 billion in new warehouses, I think the likelihood of a new special dividend announcement increases.

I believe this event is even more probable because a federal court judge has ordered the U.S. federal government to start refunding importers for the tariffs that the Supreme Court struck down. If this happens, Costco's cash position will benefit even more.

Surely, Costco shows that in the U.S. spending fatigue is a reality, since its comparable sales grew only by a couple of percentage points. But this is also a strength of Costco. In fact, when we look at the gross margin, we read that Costco's was only 11%, meaning that the company was able to keep its markup very low while focusing on delivering value. This results in customer retention and membership renewal, which is at ATHs: in the U.S., we have a renewal rate of 92.1%. But even within the membership ecosystem, there is one layer that matters more than the other. Executive members account for over 75% of Costco's sales. There are 40 million active memberships out of 147.2 million cardholders and 82.1 million memberships. This shows how a little less than 50% of Costco's members account for over three-quarters of its sales.

Costco's Valuation

When we deal with Costco, eventually all discussions end with its valuation. No one questions Costco's quality, and many truly admire the company for how it has grown, but many are reluctant to buy it because of its multiples. Indeed, Costco's valuation makes many raise their eyebrows.

We see that the company is back to a 50x fwd P/E, a fwd EV/Ebitda of 30.7x, and a fwd P/FCF of 33.5x. The PEG ratio is 5x, which is the consequence of a fwd P/E of around 50x and an expected earnings growth of around 10%. Even in the case of a special dividend, the stock would remain overvalued, as these multiples suggest.

Costco is a great business, but I am not buying it at this valuation. It simply is too expensive to justify stepping in with the risk of seeing the stock trade flat for another year or two or even selling off during an unexpected market downturn.

Great company, but the rating remains neutral.

data.title: Intercontinental Exchange: Current Multiples Outpace Earnings Growth
data.description:
Analysis

Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. (ICE) currently trades at a price-to-earnings ratio of 28x on a TTM basis. The return on equity on an unadjusted basis is around 11%, however, the interesting aspect is that this figure comes after taking into account roughly $30 billion of goodwill on the books. When adjusting for that, the return on equity metric would change altogether to a very high number. Nevertheless, on the basis of the current earnings profile—ICE at the current price of $164 with EPS of $5.77 doesn't look attractive as an investment allocation.

The current no-growth value amounts to around $60 when discounted at the rate of 10%. (The FY25 diluted per share earning was $5.77) The remaining component in the current price is attributable to growth prospects. There are a few developments happening, without a doubt, but I do not see any material significant catalyst that can materially move the needle for ICE, which is at above $9.5 billion in revenues.

Also, it is important to note that return on capital metrics are not usually a good way to analyze asset-light businesses such as Intercontinental Exchange. Exchanges like ICE do not necessarily have to deploy capital in order to generate incremental earnings, since the economics of the business are largely driven by network effects and transaction volumes rather than capital employed. Businesses like ICE are therefore better evaluated on their earning power and the durability of those earnings.

I recently covered Interactive Brokers (IBKR), which is also involved in capital markets and is currently trading at approximately the same earnings multiple, while the price-to-book was nearly twice that of Intercontinental Exchange. Still, I considered that an investment proposition and rated it a soft buy. The case could be made here as well—however, the differentiation lies in the growth prospects of the two companies.

ICE is now more of a mature, stable business—less cyclical and more predictable, but at the same time more capped in terms of outsized business performance. Therefore, at the current price of around $164, with TTM earnings around $5.8, the stock is rated a hold for investors who have held the position for quite some time. For others, I do not see any particularly compelling thesis at the current juncture.

Shareholder Return vs Earnings Growth

In terms of shareholder returns, ICE has outperformed the growth in EPS. While the earnings CAGR would come to around 9%, the total shareholder return would come to roughly 14.5% over the last 10 years.

The growth rate in EPS being higher than book value is also the result of dividends paid by ICE. This had resulted in total-return being greater by roughly 1.5% than the price return over the period, approximately in line with the company’s dividend yield.

While the total shareholder return of 14.5% is decent, the S&P 500 has delivered almost similar returns over the same period. This does not diminish the company’s performance, but it does suggest that the outcome is rather moderate.

This is more so because a meaningful portion of these returns have been driven by market re-rating and multiple expansion rather than underlying earnings growth. As reflected in the chart above, price returns have exceeded the growth in EPS, indicating that a significant portion of the shareholder returns are attributable to higher valuation multiples rather than expansion in the business itself.

Absence of Material Catalyst
