Although Intercontinental Exchange is expanding into newer market segments such as prediction markets, developing technological initiatives, and operational synergies, with trading volumes also being at elevated levels, nevertheless, when considering the overall scale of the company’s operations, most of these developments appear to be routine progress necessary to manage and operate the business. Contrary to what many might infer, they do not appear significant enough to materially move either revenues or earnings for the company as a whole.

The other factor usually discussed is significant developments in the mortgage technology segment. However, the segment is inherently cyclical, and interest rate fluctuations could materially alter the prospects. Even if one assumes that the developments proceed as expected and result in higher growth rates in the mortgage segment, when we account for around 50% of revenue attributable to the exchange segment—largely in the form of transaction income dependent on trading activity—it becomes difficult to argue that any other single vertical would materially enhance the company’s value at a multiple of around 30× earnings. A similar argument applies to other remaining standalone segments as well.

The case attributed to increased activity in energy markets does make sense, but I believe the rise in near-term volatility leading to higher trading, speculative, and hedging requirements is more of a short-term phenomenon than a structural change, since the segment itself is already fairly mature.

I believe the current multiples already incorporate the possibility of above-average growth in the non-exchange segments of ICE, and therefore developments that may appear promising may not move the needle significantly for the company.

Again, this remains somewhat subjective—someone might consider these developments more important than what I am suggesting, and that could be correct. The point, eventually, is how much earnings or cash flow growth would flow to the business overall in the end, and what effect that would have on the overall basis.

At-least to me, the business developments appear gradual rather than transformational, and not a meaningful catalyst for the company in the near term that could materially alter its earnings trajectory. Nevertheless, it would do good to track of major announcements, including larger mergers and acquisitions, and revisit the case if and when they are out in public.

PeerValuation Comparison

ICE, in terms of valuation, is mostly similar to other listed exchanges such as Nasdaq and CME. Profitability measures are not much different either. Looking at comparable peers, ICE at this time does not appear to be meaningfully differentiated from other exchanges to support either a buy or even a short thesis.

In essence, any outperformance would depend on future developments and catalysts, which, in my view, would more or less be relevant to the whole sector, whether positive or negative. Looking at the peer group further supports the assertion of an absence of meaningful price discrepancies in terms of relative valuations as well to support a long investment case.

Valuation Opinion

As I have mentioned at the start, the current valuations are not attractive enough to make ICE a compelling investment proposition.

With FY25 diluted EPS of $5.77, the current market price of around $164 implies a trailing multiple of roughly 28.5× earnings. This is fairly optimistic rather than conservative, and suggests the market already incorporates many of the anticipated positive prospects for the company's developments, as certainty. This doesn’t leave much room for further re-rating either.

Furthermore, both the price-to-earnings and price-to-book multiples are above their 10-year average ranges, even if not at the absolute highs. This is, relevant considering the much larger scale of the company’s operations today, compared with when the business was smaller in operations

Kindly note, companies such as Intercontinental Exchange should be valued primarily on their earning power, which is less contingent on the balance sheet value of assets held and more on the scale and network of operations. I have included price-to-book merely to visualize the change in tandem with price-to-earnings. However, whether the stock appears expensive or cheap rests largely on current earning power prospects.

The company guides to mid-single-digit recurring revenue growth across its core exchange and data businesses, with mortgage technology expected to grow in similar range, suggesting that the company’s long-term growth profile remains relatively moderate. Transaction revenue, when adjusted for cyclicality, does not appear to offer materially higher growth prospects either. Historically, the company has grown net diluted earnings from $2.39 to $5.77, implying roughly a 9% earnings CAGR, which again reflects the steady, but not exceptional growth.

Basically, unless someone has any noteworthy reason to believe that earnings can grow at a CAGR meaningfully higher than the current guidance and the previous 10-year rate over the coming decade, while also making an allowance for multiple compression—this becomes a rather suboptimal allocation.

The Seeking Alpha quant rating assigns an “F” to the stock in terms of valuation. That might appear a little severe, but valuations are nonetheless far from compelling at the current price.

Risk Aspects

ICE, operates in the capital markets segment and is therefore exposed to various macro factors and the broader global economic outlook. For example, a rise in interest rates can negatively affect its mortgage segment, while a reduction in trading volumes across energy, equities, and futures markets could cause a dip in earnings.

Further, the exchange has to constantly monitor and update its risk management frameworks, margin requirements, and clearing safeguards. Any lapse could expose the company to adverse operational consequences. Technology-related risk factors—such as system glitches, hacking threats, data protection & integrity issues, and similar vulnerabilities—if not managed properly, can also lead to operational disruptions.

Also, competition from other exchange operators and alternative trading venues is always present. In addition, regulatory oversight cannot be ignored, and the possibility of regulatory changes leading to the loss of a major revenue line is always present.

Most importantly, at the current multiple of around 30× earnings, any compression in market multiples could might lead to material underperformance in the company’s share price.

Takeaway

The company is operationally decent, without a doubt. Nevertheless, at the current price-to-earnings multiple of around 30×, this is not a viable investment proposition.

The reason I am not rating it as a sell is because operationally there is nothing wrong with the company. In fact, as a business franchise, it is very strong. The company is meant to work like this—a stable business with predictable economics, an easy-to-understand asset-light business model, strong cash flow generation, and extraordinary returns on tangible equity, and so on.

The only demerit is that the price being paid is quite higher than the value offered. There might not be a need to sell if someone holds this as part of a core portfolio. Nevertheless, an investor purchasing at the current price stands a risk of underperforming.

Therefore, it is rated as a hold only for long-term investors who bought the stock some years ago. For fresh allocations, I consider it an avoid for now.
